title: The problem generics solve
instruction:
Without generics, you'd write a separate `getFirst` function for every type — `getFirstNumber`, `getFirstString`, etc. Generics let you write it **once** and have TypeScript figure out the type at the call site.

Run this and notice that each call returns the correct type automatically.
successMessage: T is inferred separately for each call — `number` for scores, `string` for names. You wrote the logic once and TypeScript handled the types. That's what generics are for.

title: Generic API response wrapper
instruction:
A common real-world pattern: every API endpoint returns a response with the same shape (`success`, `data`, `error`) but different `data` types. A generic wrapper models this perfectly.

This is the pattern used in virtually every TypeScript API client.
successMessage: One `ApiResponse<T>` handles users, orders, products — any type. And TypeScript knows that `userResult.data` is `User | null`, so it enforces the null check. Real codebases use this pattern constantly.

title: Generic constraints — only accept what you need
instruction:
Sometimes you want generics but need to guarantee certain properties exist. Use `extends` to constrain what types are allowed.

Build a function that works on any object with an `id` — without caring about the rest of the object.
successMessage: `T extends HasId` means TypeScript guarantees `.id` exists, so `.find(item => item.id === id)` compiles. But you didn't restrict T to only having an id — it can carry any other fields too.

title: Multiple type parameters
instruction:
Functions can have multiple type parameters that are inferred independently. This is useful for transformations, pairings, and mappings.

Build a `groupBy` function that works with any array and any key.
successMessage: Two type parameters: `T` for the item type, `K` for the key type — both inferred automatically. `groupBy` works for employees, products, orders, anything.
